vulnerabilities.title: Locking Outside Active Lockdrop Period Zeroes Stored Remainder
vulnerabilities.summary: In _lock, the local variable remainder is initialized to 0. The block that computes the new remainder only executes during an active lockdrop. Outside the lockdrop period, remainder stays 0. At the end of _lock, lockedToken.remainder is unconditionally overwritten with this local variable, destroying any previously accumulated remainder from a lockdrop.

description.desc: uint256 remainder; (defaults to 0) at line 315; conditional NFT calculation at lines 324-335; unconditional lockedToken.remainder = remainder at line 343

vulnerabilities.impact: Any user who accumulated a non-zero remainder during a lockdrop and subsequently locks additional tokens outside a lockdrop period will permanently lose their accumulated partial-NFT credit. The maximum loss per user per token is nftCost - 1 tokens worth of NFT progress. This silently destroys value without any user-visible error or warning. Users who are active lockers (adding to their positions between lockdrops) are most affected.

vulnerabilities.title: lockOnBehalf Can Overwrite Recipient's Lock Duration Preference
vulnerabilities.summary: In _lock, if the lock recipient's playerSettings.lockDuration is 0 (never set by the user), _lockDuration falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration. At the end of _lock, playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration is unconditionally set to this value. An attacker can use lockOnBehalf to force-set a victim's lock duration to the lockdrop minimum before the victim sets their own preference.
vulnerabilities.impact: The direct impact is that an attacker can set a victim's lock duration to lockdrop.minLockDuration before the victim sets their own preference. This affects subsequent lock operations for the victim. The victim may unknowingly lock with a shorter duration than intended, potentially receiving fewer rewards. While the victim can call setLockDuration to correct this, the unauthorized modification of settings is a real code defect. The severity is medium because recovery is possible and the impact is limited to settings manipulation rather than direct fund loss.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20 unlock may silently fail or revert for non-standard tokens due to missing SafeERC20 handling
vulnerabilities.summary: The contract uses IERC20.transfer/transferFrom directly without SafeERC20. Tokens that return no boolean (or return false instead of reverting) can cause reverts (ABI decode) or silent failure, resulting in incorrect accounting: lockedToken.quantity is decreased but user may not receive tokens (if token returns false but doesn’t revert).

description.desc: IERC20(_tokenContract).transferFrom(...) and IERC20(_tokenContract).transfer(...) used without SafeERC20

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - If a non-standard ERC20 token is configured by admin: (1) DoS: All users' funds locked in that token become permanently trapped, as both lock and unlock operations revert. (2) Accounting desync: For false-returning tokens, users may have their locked quantity reduced without receiving tokens. The defect exists in the code regardless of current token configuration and represents a latent risk that materializes upon admin action.

vulnerabilities.title: USD price proposal cannot execute when APPROVE_THRESHOLD == 1 because proposeUSDPrice never calls _execUSDPriceUpdate() after auto-approval
vulnerabilities.summary: proposeUSDPrice increments approvalsCount and records the proposer approval, but does not call _execUSDPriceUpdate(). Therefore, even if APPROVE_THRESHOLD is set to 1, the proposal will not be executed until a separate approveUSDPrice call occurs, effectively making the minimum approvals 2 and breaking admin-configured governance semantics.

description.desc: proposeUSDPrice sets approvalsCount++ but does not call _execUSDPriceUpdate()

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect breaks governance semantics when APPROVE_THRESHOLD is configured to 1. Price updates that should execute immediately upon proposal would require a second operator, potentially causing delayed price updates in emergency scenarios. If only one operator is available (e.g., emergency situation), the price update is completely blocked. Stale prices can distort getLockedWeightedValue calculations, affecting reward distribution for all users.

vulnerabilities.title: setLockDuration Bypass Allows Immediate Withdrawal of Time-Locked Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: The setLockDuration function validates new unlock time using `block.timestamp + _duration >= old unlockTime` but computes the actual new unlockTime as `lastLockTime + _duration`. As time passes since lastLockTime, the gap between block.timestamp and lastLockTime widens, allowing users to pass the validation check with a small duration that sets unlockTime to a past timestamp, enabling immediate withdrawal.

description.desc: Validation uses `uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_duration) < unlockTime` but assignment uses `lastLockTime + uint32(_duration)` — these diverge as time passes

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Every locked position in the contract is vulnerable once sufficient time has elapsed (past the midpoint of the lock duration). Users can: (1) withdraw locked tokens far before their committed unlock time, (2) retain any lockdrop NFT rewards that were granted based on the original lock commitment, (3) immediately re-lock with a new long duration to farm more NFT rewards and repeat. This completely undermines the time-lock mechanism and lockdrop reward integrity. Total TVL of locked tokens is at risk of premature withdrawal.

vulnerabilities.title: lockOnBehalf Enables Permanent Fund Freezing via Zero-Cost Unlock Timer Reset
vulnerabilities.summary: The _lock function unconditionally overwrites unlockTime to `block.timestamp + lockDuration` for the recipient. Any user can call lockOnBehalf with _quantity=0 (or 1 wei for ETH) for any victim, resetting their unlockTime to the full lock duration from the current timestamp. Repeated calls permanently prevent the victim from ever withdrawing their locked funds.

description.desc: _lock unconditionally sets `lockedToken.unlockTime = uint32(block.timestamp) + uint32(_lockDuration)` without checking if new unlockTime > old unlockTime for existing locks, and lockOnBehalf permits any caller

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Any user's locked funds can be permanently frozen by any other user at near-zero cost. The entire protocol TVL is at risk. The attack also destroys victims' accumulated NFT remainder values. This directly violates the stated invariant that 'Funds must not get locked forever.' The attack is trivially automatable and undetectable without off-chain monitoring.

vulnerabilities.title: Zero Lock Duration Enables Instant Lock/Unlock Cycle for Reward Gaming
vulnerabilities.summary: When lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no lockdrop configured or stale lockdrop with minLockDuration=0) and a user has not set their own lockDuration, the effective _lockDuration defaults to 0. This sets unlockTime = block.timestamp, allowing immediate unlock within the same block. Since forceHarvest is called in both lock and unlock, the user can accumulate schnibble rewards without meaningful time commitment.

description.desc: _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration; if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; } — both can be 0, leading to unlockTime = block.timestamp

vulnerabilities.impact: The code defect allows zero-duration locks, violating the fundamental assumption that locked tokens must be committed for a meaningful period. Impact depends on forceHarvest implementation: if rewards are time-based (per-second), impact may be minimal. If any per-call or per-lock component exists, rewards could be extracted without commitment. Additionally, getLockedWeightedValue returns non-zero during the brief locked period, which could affect external consumers. The defect also allows gaming via flash loans - lock large amounts, trigger forceHarvest with inflated getLockedWeightedValue, unlock immediately.

vulnerabilities.title: USD Price Governance Vote Exclusivity Violation Enables Double-Counting and Stuck Proposals
vulnerabilities.summary: The disapproveUSDPrice function checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` to prevent approvers from disapproving, but approveUSDPrice does NOT check `disapprovals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId`. A price feed operator can disapprove first, then subsequently approve the same proposal, incrementing both counters. This breaks vote mutual exclusivity and can cause proposals to become permanently stuck.

description.desc: approveUSDPrice checks `approvals[msg.sender] == _usdProposalId` but never checks `disapprovals[msg.sender]`; disapproveUSDPrice sets disapprovals[msg.sender] but doesn't block later approval

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code defect breaks vote mutual exclusivity (INV-ACCESS-002), which is a clear logic error. The most severe claimed impact (stuck proposals) is not achievable due to immediate deletion on disapproval threshold, but the defect still undermines governance integrity: (1) Vote counts can exceed the number of unique operators, (2) Disapproval votes become partially meaningless since operators can switch to approval afterwards, (3) The semantic meaning of disapproval is weakened as it doesn't preclude later approval.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Insolvency Leading to Last-Withdrawer Loss
vulnerabilities.summary: The _lock function records `lockedToken.quantity += _quantity` using the requested transfer amount rather than the actual received amount. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded. Over time, total recorded quantities exceed the actual contract balance, causing later unlockers to be unable to withdraw their full recorded balance.

description.desc: token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) followed by lockedToken.quantity += _quantity — no balance delta check

vulnerabilities.impact: If a fee-on-transfer token is configured: (1) Progressive insolvency grows with each lock operation by feeRate * _quantity per lock. (2) Last users to unlock permanently lose their tokens. (3) During lockdrop periods, users receive more NFT reveals than they should based on actual tokens deposited. (4) The getLockedWeightedValue function returns inflated values, potentially over-rewarding users in external reward calculations. The severity is HIGH if fee-on-transfer tokens are ever configured; the code defect exists regardless of current configuration.

vulnerabilities.title: USD price proposal can become unremovable and unexecutable due to lack of any expiry/cancel path
vulnerabilities.summary: Once a USDUpdateProposal exists, there is no time-based expiry, no admin cancellation, and no proposer cancellation. If approvals never reach APPROVE_THRESHOLD and disapprovals never reach DISAPPROVE_THRESHOLD, the proposal persists indefinitely and blocks new proposals (proposeUSDPrice reverts when proposer != address(0)). A single PriceFeed operator can grief by proposing and then the operator set can simply stop interacting, freezing all future USD updates.

description.desc: proposeUSDPrice/approve/disapprove require proposal state but no cancel/expiry function exists; proposeUSDPrice reverts if usdUpdateProposal.proposer != address(0)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. USD price staleness affects getLockedWeightedValue() which is used by external contracts (likely AccountManager) for reward calculations. Stale prices cause incorrect weighted value calculations, potentially over- or under-rewarding players. The inability to update prices is permanent unless the admin can rotate PriceFeed roles to bring in new operators who haven't yet voted — but even this workaround relies on operational procedures not encoded in the contract. The code itself has no safety valve.

vulnerabilities.title: USD Price Feed Distortion via Single Price Array Application
vulnerabilities.summary: The proposeUSDPrice function takes an array of token contracts but only a single USD price. When executed, this single price is applied to all tokens in the array, potentially causing massive mispricing if tokens with different values are bundled.

description.desc: A single proposedPrice is applied in a loop to all tokenContract addresses in the proposal array

vulnerabilities.impact: If WETH ($3000) and USDC ($1) are bundled in a single proposal: setting price to $3000 would make USDC holders appear 3000x wealthier in weighted value calculations; setting price to $1 would make WETH holders appear 3000x poorer. This directly impacts schnibbles emission rates and reward distribution through getLockedWeightedValue. The distortion persists until a new proposal corrects it, during which time rewards are unfairly distributed. The code also allows only one active proposal at a time, meaning correcting the mispricing for one token delays updates for others.

vulnerabilities.title: Phantom Remainder After Full Unlock Enables Discounted NFT Minting on Subsequent Lock
vulnerabilities.summary: When a user fully unlocks their tokens via unlock(), lockedToken.remainder is never cleared (only quantity is decremented). This leaves a phantom remainder referencing value the user already withdrew. On the next lockdrop, calling lock(token, smallAmount) causes _lock to compute quantity = smallAmount + phantom_remainder, granting NFTs based on the combined value. The user effectively receives NFTs at a discount proportional to the phantom remainder since those tokens are no longer locked.

description.desc: unlock() decrements lockedToken.quantity but never clears lockedToken.remainder; _lock() line 275 computes quantity = _quantity + lockedToken.remainder, including phantom remainder from already-withdrawn tokens in NFT calculation

vulnerabilities.impact: Every user who accumulates remainder during a lockdrop and then fully unlocks retains phantom remainder. This phantom remainder provides a discount on future NFT minting. The discount per NFT equals phantom_remainder/nftCost * 100%. In the worst case (remainder = nftCost - 1), a user can mint an NFT for just 1 token. This dilutes NFT distribution for honest participants and violates the economic invariant INV-ECON-002 that remainder must not exceed locked quantity.

vulnerabilities.title: ETH Locked via lock() by Smart Contract Wallets Permanently Frozen Due to .transfer() Gas Limit
vulnerabilities.summary: The lock() function accepts ETH deposits from any caller including smart contract wallets (Gnosis Safe proxies, account abstraction wallets, etc.). However, unlock() returns ETH using payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity) which forwards only 2300 gas. Smart contract wallets whose receive/fallback functions require more than 2300 gas will fail to receive ETH, permanently locking their funds with no alternative withdrawal path.

description.desc: payable(msg.sender).transfer(_quantity) in unlock uses 2300 gas stipend; no alternative ETH withdrawal method exists

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any ETH locked by smart contract wallets with complex receive/fallback functions (Gnosis Safe, many AA wallets, multisigs) is permanently frozen. The total value at risk equals the sum of all ETH locked by such wallets. Given the deployment on Blast where smart contract wallet usage is common, this could affect a significant portion of users. The author's own specification states 'Funds must not get locked forever', making this a direct violation of stated invariants. There is no admin rescue mechanism, no alternative withdrawal path, and no way to recover the funds.

vulnerabilities.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS in Core Functions due to Unbounded Token Array
vulnerabilities.summary: The `configuredTokenContracts` array grows unboundedly with each new token configuration. Functions like `setLockDuration` and `getLockedWeightedValue` iterate over this entire array, leading to a permanent DoS if the array becomes too large.

description.desc: `for (uint256 i; i < configuredTokensLength; i++)` iterating over unbounded array

vulnerabilities.impact: If the array grows sufficiently large: (1) setLockDuration becomes permanently uncallable, preventing users from updating their lock durations. (2) getLockedWeightedValue becomes uncallable, potentially breaking external reward distribution contracts. (3) getLocked becomes uncallable, breaking UI/frontend integrations. Recovery requires contract upgrade since there is no removal function. Severity is HIGH for the code defect itself, though practical exploitation likelihood is LOW-MEDIUM given the admin-only nature of token configuration.

vulnerabilities.title: getLockedWeightedValue may revert or overflow with misconfigured decimals/large values, breaking downstream reward distribution if used on-chain
vulnerabilities.summary: getLockedWeightedValue computes deltaDecimal = 10**(18 - decimals). If decimals > 18, subtraction underflows and reverts. Additionally, (deltaDecimal * quantity * usdPrice) can overflow before division. If any critical state-changing function in other contracts calls getLockedWeightedValue internally (e.g., during reward distribution), this can halt protocol operations.

description.desc: deltaDecimal = 10 ** (18 - configuredTokens[token].decimals); multiplication before division may overflow

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code defect is real (missing input validation + unsafe arithmetic), but exploitation requires admin misconfiguration. Impact if triggered: getLockedWeightedValue reverts for players holding the misconfigured token. If this view function is used in state-changing paths in external contracts (not verifiable from provided code), it could block reward claims or period transitions. Recovery requires admin action to reconfigure the token.
